titleStmt.title: A Perspective on Plasticity, Dissipation and the 2 nd Law of Thermodynamics 1

analytic.title: A Perspective on Plasticity, Dissipation and the 2 nd Law of Thermodynamics 1

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: The requirement of a non-negative dissipation rate for all possible deformation histories is generally imposed on plastic constitutive relations. This is a constraint analogous to the Coleman-Noll [1] postulate that the Clausius-Duhem inequality needs to be satisfied for all possible deformation histories. The physical basis for the Clausius-Duhem inequality is as a statistical limit for a large number of discrete events for a long time and is not a fundamental physical requirement for small systems for a short time. The relation between the requirement of a non-negative dissipation rate and the Clausius-Duhem inequality is considered. The consequences of imposing a non-negative dissipation rate for all possible deformation histories are illustrated for: (i) a single crystal plasticity framework that accounts for elastic lattice curvature changes as well as elastic lattice straining; and (ii) for discrete defect theories of plasticity, with attention specifically on discrete dislocation plasticity for crystalline solids and discrete shear transformation zone (STZ) plasticity for amorphous solids. Possible less restrictive conditions on the evolution of dissipation in plasticity formulations are considered as are implications for stability. The focus is on open questions and issues.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Theories of plasticity, as opposed to theories of thermoplasticity, are based on conservation laws (momentum, mass and energy) that only involve deformation measures, stress measures and internal variables. Temperature only enters through the possible dependence of material parameters on a temperature that is related to the evolution of plastic dissipation.

The requirement of a non-negative dissipation rate is a thermodynamic-like requirement analogous to the 2nd law of thermodynamics as expressed by the Clausius-Duhem inequality. However, the Clausius-Duhem inequality differs from the basic conservation laws in that it emerges as a statistical limit for a large number of discrete events over a long time and has a probability less than one of being satisfied when the the process under consideration involves discrete events that take place over a sufficiently small region for a sufficiently short time, see e.g. Evans and Searle [2], Jarzynski [3]. Most investigations where a violation of the Clausius-Duhem inequality has occurred have been at the atomistic scale, e.g. Evans et al. [4], Wang et al. [5]. However, Ostoja-Starzewski and Laudani [6] observed a violation of the Clausius-Duhem inequality in macro scale discrete particle simulations of a granular solid.

Despite these observations, for continuum formulations at any scale, the Coleman-Noll [1] postulate that requires the Clausius-Duhem inequality to be satisfied for all possible deformation histories is typically imposed. At least in some contexts, satisfying this postulate provides a stability condition, Coleman and Mizel [7], Dafermos [8]. In developing plastic constitutive relations, analogous to the Coleman-Noll [1] postulate, the plastic dissipation rate is generally required to be non-negative for all possible deformation histories.

At the micron scale, plastic deformation almost always, if not always, is a consequence of the evolution of processes that are discrete in both space and time, such as discrete dislocations for crystal plasticity and discrete shear transformation zones (STZs) for plasticity of amorphous solids. Imposing the requirement of a non-negative dissipation rate for the evolution of individual discrete defects can lead to strong restrictions, that are not consistent with atomistic modeling and/or experiment, see e.g. Needleman [9].

These observations suggest that imposing the requirement of a non-negative dissipation rate for all possible deformation histories on continuum formulations of plasticity may, in general, be overly restrictive. To provide a perspective on this possibility, the following questions are considered:
1. To what extent does the requirement of a non-negative dissipation rate emerge from the 2nd law of thermodynamics?
2. What are the consequences of imposing the requirement of a non-negative dissipation rate on a phenomenological theory of crystal plasticity?
3. What are the consequences of imposing the requirement of a non-negative dissipation rate on discrete defect plasticity theories?

4. For both phenomenological and discrete defect theories of plasticity, what are the consequences of imposing the stronger requirement that the plastic dissipation rate is non-negative for all possible deformation histories?
5. What are the consequences for stability when the dissipation rate is negative?
To begin, statements of the first and second laws of thermodynamics are presented.

div.head: The 1 st law
div.p: Attention is confined to small deformation kinematics with quasi-static deformation histories with the only loading being imposed surface tractions and/or imposed surface displacements. Energy densities are written per unit reference volume rather than per unit mass. The focus is on a purely mechanical formulation, i.e. the independent field variables entering the formulation are stress and displacement, along with their time and spatial derivatives (such as strain and strain rate), and possible constitutive internal variables. The conservation laws of continuum mechanics -conservation of mass, conservation of linear momentum; conservation of angular momentum, and conservation of energy -hold for all points of the body and for all time. In particular, for a purely mechanical formulation and quasi-static deformation histories, conservation of energy (the 1 st law) for the body requires
div.formula: Ẇ = Φ + Ḋ (1
div.p: where W is the mechanical work input, Φ is the energy stored in the body, D is the material dissipation in the body and (˙) = ∂( )/∂t. Eq. ( 1) is presumed to hold for all times.
The quantities W , Φ and D can be calculated for a body and for any sub-volume of that body in terms of volume densities so that
div.formula: W = V wdV , Φ = V ϕdV , D = V ξdV (2)
div.p: Here, V is the volume of the body or of the sub-volume. If Eq. ( 1) holds for any sub-volume of the body
for all points x k of the body and for all times.

3 The 2 nd law Consideration of the 2 nd law requires the introduction of entropy and absolute temperature which are assumed to be well defined even though plastic deformation processes are not necessarily thermodynamic equilibrium processes. The second law of thermodynamics is taken to be expressed by the Clausius inequality2 , see e.g. Rivlin [10], which states that the change in entropy, ∆S, between times t 1 and t 2 in a body with volume V is greater than or equal to the time integral of the rate of heat input into the material divided by the absolute temperature. Hence, in terms of the dissipation rate ξ which is the heat output from the material ∆S ≥
div.formula: t 2 t 1 V - ξ Θ dV dt (4
div.p: where Θ is the absolute temperature. From conservation of energy, Eq. ( 3), ∆S ≥ -
div.formula: t 2 t 1 V ẇ - φ Θ dV dt(5)
div.p: The entropy change per unit volume, ∆s, is defined at each point of the body by
div.formula: ∆S = V ∆sdV(6)
div.p: so that if Eq. ( 5) holds for every sub-volume of the body, then ∆s ≥ -
div.formula: t 2 t 1 ẇ - φ Θ dt(7)
div.p: holds at each point x k in the body. Note that satisfaction of Eq. ( 7) at all points of the body is a more restrictive condition than satisfaction of Eq. ( 5).
When Eq. ( 7) holds for any time interval

which is the Clausius-Duhem inequality. The Coleman-Noll [1] postulate is that Eq. ( 8) holds for all points in a body for all times for any possible loading history. This puts the Clausius-Duhem inequality on the same footing as the basic conservation laws of continuum mechanics even though the Clausius-Duhem inequality only emerges as a statistical limit of a large number of discrete events for a long time, see e.g. Evans and Searle [2], Jarzynski [3].

In addition to, or instead of, thermal entropy, configurational entropy (and configurational temperature) can be included in a thermodynamic formulation, see for example Bouchbinder and Langer [11], Langer et al. [12], Falk and Langer [13], McDowell [14,15]. Indeed, it is possible that configurational entropy plays a larger role than thermal entropy in some plastic dissipation processes.
In a purely mechanical formulation, entropy and temperature do not enter, and the condition analogous to the Clausius-Duhem inequality, Eq. ( 8), is

Eq. ( 9) follows from the Clausius-Duhem inequality, Eq. ( 8), if ṡΘ = 0, i.e. if either the entropy rate ṡ = 0 or the absolute temperature Θ = 0. However: (i) setting Θ = 0 in Eq. ( 8) is problematic because Θ is in the denominator in Eq. ( 7); and (ii) setting ṡ = 0 for an irreversible process involving dissipation such as the evolution of plastic deformation is questionable from a thermodynamic perspective. With ṡΘ ≥ 0, satisfaction of Eq. ( 9) implies satisfaction of Eq. ( 8) and then satisfaction of Eq. ( 9) is a sufficient, but not necessary, condition for satisfaction of Eq. ( 8), Integrating Eq. ( 9) over the volume of the body gives

If ξ ≥ 0 at all points of a body then Ḋ ≥ 0. Eq. ( 10) is less restrictive than Eqs. (9) because Eq. ( 10) can be satisfied with ξ negative at some points and non-negative at other points. Also, from Eq. ( 5)
div.formula: V ẇ - φ Θ dV = 1 Θ Ẇ -Φ if only if Θ = constant in V(11)
div.p: Hence, the left hand side of Eq. ( 10) follows directly from the volume integral in Eq. ( 5) only if the temperature Θ is constant in V .

Possible restrictions that can be imposed to preclude a negative dissipation rate in a purely mechanical formulation include: (i) ξ ≥ 0 for all points of the body; (ii) ξ ≥ 0 for all possible deformation histories (analogous to the Coleman-Noll [1] postulate); and (iii) Ḋ ≥ 0 for the body.

div.head: Continuum slip crystal plasticity
div.p: The constraint imposed by Eq, ( 9) is illustrated for a continuum slip crystal plasticity formulation. Plastic deformation is represented by slips on specific lattice planes in specific lattice directions. The combination of a slip direction and a slip plane normal is termed a slip system. The total deformation is comprised of elastic deformation of the crystal lattice and plastic deformation arising due to slip on the specified slip systems. Only the plastic deformation due to slip gives rise to dissipation.

Following Nye [16], the elastic deformation of the lattice is taken to involve both lattice strain and lattice curvature. The stored energy per unit volume, ϕ, is taken to depend on the lattice strain ϵ e ij and the change in lattice curvature κ e ij . In principle, ϕ could also depend on the magnitude of slip on each slip system. That would lead to additional terms in equations involving the time rate of change of stored energy, but the plastic dissipation rate expression would not be affected. Hence, φ is given by
div.formula: φ(ϵ e ij , κ e ij ) = ∂ϕ ∂ϵ e ij εe ij + ∂ϕ ∂κ e ij κe ij(12)
div.p: The lattice curvature components, κ e ij are given by the spatial gradient of the lattice rotation vector so that κ e ij is not symmetric, i.e. κ e ij ̸ = κ e ji , Nye [16]. From Eq. ( 3), the work rate per unit volume can be written as
div.formula: ẇ = φ + ξ = σ ji εe ij + M ji κe ij + ξ(13)
div.p: where σ ji is the work conjugate stress and M ji is the work conjugate bending moment. From Eqs. ( 12) and ( 13),
div.formula: σ ji = ∂ϕ ∂ϵ e ij , M ji = ∂ϕ ∂κ e ij(14)
div.p: In continuum slip crystal plasticity, the deformation rate due to slip is given by
div.formula: up ij = β γ(β) s (β) i m (β) j(15)
div.p: Here, γ(β) is the slip rate, s
div.formula: (β) i
div.p: is the slip direction and m (β) j
is the slip plane normal for slip system β.
The total strain rate, εij , is
div.formula: εij = εe ij + εp ij = εe ij + 1 2 up ij + up ji (16
div.p: with up ij given by Eq. (15). A plastic constitutive relation specifies γ(β) as a function of stress state and internal variables. Whether the constitutive relation is rate independent or rate dependent is irrelevant for the present purpose.
From Eqs. ( 15) and ( 16)
div.formula: σ ji εe ij = σ ji εij - β s (β) i σ ji m (β) j γ(β) = σ ji εij - β τ (β) γ(β)(17)
div.p: where
div.formula: τ (β) = s (β) i σ ji m (β) j
div.p: is the Schmid resolved shear stress on slip system β. Because plastic deformation is only due to slip, the dissipation rate can be written as
div.formula: ξ = β π (β) γ(β)(18)
div.p: The requirement that the dissipation rate at each point x k of the body is non-negative, Eq. ( 9), takes the form ξ(
A less restrictive requirement is
div.formula: Ḋ = V π (β) γ(β) dV ≥ 0 (20
div.p: with V the volume of the body.

In developing crystal plasticity constitutive relations, the requirement typically imposed is that π (β) γ(β) ≥ 0 for each β. However, crystal structures/slip modes may be possible where at least one π (β) γ(β) < 0 but ξ ≥ 0 so that this requirement could be overly restrictive for satisfying Eq. (19).

Nye [16] (Appendix D) showed that, for a solid with dislocations, a closed square lattice circuit in the undeformed lattice has a closure failure in the deformed curved lattice denoted by the vector B i due to dislocation induced lattice curvature,. Nye [16] also showed that B i is the net Burgers vector of the dislocations inside a closed circuit of unit area with normal ℓ i and is given by
div.formula: B i = α ij ℓ j(21)
div.p: Nye's dislocation density tensor, α ij , and the lattice curvature tensor κ e ij are related by, Nye [16],
div.formula: κ e ij = α ji - 1 2 α nn δ ji(22)
div.p: where δ ji is the Kronecker delta. Within the context of small deformation continuum slip plasticity theory
div.formula: α ji = β e ikl γ (β) ,k s (β) j m (β) l(23)
div.p: with e ikl being the alternating tensor, Gurtin [17], Bittencourt et al. [18]. The term M ji κe ij in Eq. ( 13) can be written as
div.formula: M ji κe ij = M ji αji - 1 2 M nn αnn = β e ikl M ji s (β) j m (β) l - 1 2 e mkl M nn s (β) m m (β) l γ(β) ,k = β χ (β) k γ(β) ,k(24)
div.p: where χ
div.formula: (β) k = M ji e ikl s (β) j m (β) l - 1 2 e mkl M nn s (β) m m (β) l(25)
div.p: Using Eq. ( 25), Eq. ( 13) becomes
div.formula: 3 ẇ = σ ji εe ij + β χ (β) k γ(β) ,k + β π (β) γ(β)(26)
div.p: The right hand side of Eq. ( 26) is the internal work rate per unit volume associated with Gurtin's [17] nonlocal theory of crystal plasticity with "energetic hardening" specialized to the small deformation gradient case, see also Bittencourt et al. [18]. The terms in parenthesis in Eq. ( 26) are the elastic contribution and the last term is the plastic dissipation rate. With Eq. ( 17) used to replace the term σ ji εe ij in Eq. ( 26), the work rate per unit volume expression on which the governing equations are based is obtained.

The slip rate gradients, γ(β) ,k , do not enter the expression for the dissipation rate because they only enter this formulation through their role in inducing elastic lattice bending. i.e. via Eq. (22). In this regard it is interesting to note that nonphysical responses can be obtained when a plasticity theory includes plastic strain rate gradient terms in the dissipation rate expression, see e.g. Fleck et al. [19].

div.head: Discrete defect plasticity
div.p: Plastic deformation of solids is almost always, if not always, associated with the evolution of discrete defects. Plastic deformation can then be modeled by calculating how such a collection of discrete defects evolves. Individual discrete defect events can take place over a small distance for a short time (a key issue, of course, is what constitutes "small" and what constitutes "short"). There is no fundamental reason to expect the Clausius-Duhem inequality, Eq. ( 8), to be satisfied or for the dissipation rate to be non-negative for each discrete event. Indeed, enforcing the requirement of a non-negative dissipation rate for each discrete defect can pose such a strong limit on the kinetic equations describing the evolution of a defect so as to exclude the representation of physically possible behaviors.

div.head: Discrete dislocation plasticity
div.p: The main mechanism of plastic deformation in crystalline metals at room temperature is the glide of dislocations on specific lattice planes. In discrete dislocation plasticity, the dislocations are represented as line defects in a linear elastic solid. The dislocations have a characteristic vector with the dimension of length, b, termed the Burgers vector. Plastic dissipation is mainly associated with the change in configurational energy as the dislocations glide. The dissipation rate can be written as
div.formula: Ḋ = N K=1 L K P K v K dl (27
div.p: 3 Note that an equation of the form of Eq. ( 26) is obtained when κ e ij and α ji are related by a general linear expression i.e. κ e ij = R ijkl α lk , but with a different expression for χ
div.formula: (β) k
div.p: from that in Eq. ( 25).
where N is the number of dislocations, v K is the dislocation velocity, the integration is over the dislocation line and P K , the Peach-Koehler (configurational) force, is given by
div.formula: P K = σnb + N M =1,̸ =K σ M nb |b K | (28)
div.p: Here, b K is the Burgers vector associated with dislocation K, σnb is a stress induced by the boundary conditions, σ nb is the Schmid resolved shear stress given by σ nb = bK i σ ij n K j with bK i a unit vector in the Burgers vector direction of dislocation K and |b K | the Burgers vector amplitude.
A kinetic relation for v K needs to be specified. For modeling crystals such as fcc metal crystals, a linear relation of the form
div.formula: v K = 1 C K P K (29)
div.p: is typically used where C K is the dislocation mobility. Eq. ( 29) implies that plastic flow in such a crystal only depends on the shear stress in the slip plane in the Burgers vector direction, i.e. the Schmid resolved shear stress. However, there are circumstances where there is evidence from atomistic calculations and experiment that other shear stress components can play a role; for example, in modeling dislocation glide when cross-slip occurs, e.g. Hussein et al. [20], Malka-Markovitz et al. [21], and in modeling bcc crystals as well as crystals that have a complex dislocation core structure so that shear stress components other than σ nb can affect dislocation mobility, e.g. Vitek and Paidar [22]. In such cases a possible kinetic relation that has non-Schmid shear stress dependence is of the form
div.formula: v K = 1 C K Q K = 1 C K P K + ρ K σmt + N M =1,̸ =K c M σ M mt (30
div.p: where ρ K and c M are constitutive parameters, σ mt = m K i σ ij t K j with m K i and t K j unit vectors in directions that differ from n i and/or bi .
The dissipation rate is
div.formula: Ḋ = N K=1 L K 1 C K P K Q K dl = N K=1 ḊK(31)
div.p: with C K ≥ 0 and Q K = P K , Ḋ in Eq. ( 27) is guaranteed to be non-negative. However, with C K ≥ 0 and Q K ̸ = P K there is in general no guarantee that ḊK ≥ 0. In this context, the requirement analogous to the Coleman-Noll [1] postulate is
div.formula: ḊK = L K 1 C K P K Q K dl ≥ 0(32)
div.p: for each K and all possible stress states. Discrete dislocation plasticity formulations with non-Schmid kinetic relations have been developed, e.g. by Wang and Beyerlein [23] and Chaussidon et al. [24]. Wang and Beyerlein [23] note that incorporating non-Schmid effects into the discrete dislocation formulation is critical for obtaining predictions consistent with experimental observations. The dissipation rate associated with such formulations has not been calculated so it is not known whether or not Eq. ( 32) is satisfied for the relations that have been proposed. In any case, for such dislocation processes it is possible to formulate a kinetic relation where the value of ḊK is negative for some K for a short time but non-negative otherwise.

In discrete dislocation plasticity, the most direct calculation is for ḊK . However, ξ can also be calculated as in Benzerga et al. [25], but that is a more complex calculation. If ḊK < 0 then necessarily ξ < 0 at one or more points of the body.

div.head: Discrete shear transformation plasticity
div.p: Plastic deformation of amorphous materials, such as metallic glasses, typically occurs via local atomic re-arrangements termed shear transformation zones (STZs). In the context of continuum mechanics, STZs have been modeled as transforming Eshelby [26,27] inclusions. A transforming Eshelby [26,27] inclusion is an elliptical sub-volume of a uniform linear elastic solid that undergoes a transformation such that if the inclusion were removed from the matrix, it would undergo a uniform strain ϵ * ij with zero stress. However, due to the constraint of the surrounding matrix material both the material inside the inclusion and the material outside the inclusion are stressed.
The dissipation rate Ḋ is calculated via Eq. ( 1) as, Vasoya et al. [28],
div.formula: Ḋ = N K=1 Z K ij ε * K ij (33
div.p: For a body with a collection of N STZs and with a stress field σ 0 ij (x k , t) arising from the imposed loading
div.formula: Z K ij = V K σ 0 ij (x k , t) + N J=1 σ T J ij (x k , t) dV(34)
div.p: where the integration is over the volume of STZ K and σ T J ij (x k , t) is the transformation stress associated with STZ J that is uniform in STZ J for an elliptical inclusion, Eshelby [26,27].
A non-negative dissipation rate Ḋ requires
div.formula: Ḋ = N K=1 Z K ij ε * K ij ≥ 0 (35
div.p: The requirement analogous to the Coleman-Noll [1] postulate is
div.formula: Z K ij ε * K ij ≥ 0 for each K (36)
div.p: and the condition Eq. ( 36) is satisfied if
div.formula: Z K ij = K K ijkl ε * K kl (37
div.p: where each K K ijkl is a positive definite fourth order tensor so that
div.formula: Ḋ = N K=1 ε * K ij K K ijkl ε * K kl (38)
div.p: Employing Eq. (37) for a shear transformation or a volumetric transformation limits the maximum allowable transformation strain to values that are smaller than indicated by atomistic calculations and experiment, as discussed by Vasoya et al. [28]. A kinetic equation for an Eshelby [26,27] inclusion undergoing a plane strain shear transformation that allows for a negative dissipation rate for a short time is given by Needleman [9], Eq. ( 64), that, in principle, allows for larger transformation strains to be achieved. However, whether such a relation is physically appropriate and what conditions need to be placed on the kinetic equation parameters to ensure stability are not known.
As for discrete dislocation plasticity, it is simpler to calculate ḊK than it is to calculate ξ at each point of the body.

div.head: Plasticity and a Negative Dissipation Rate
div.p: At least in some contexts, the requirement, analogous to the Coleman-Noll [1] postulate, that the dissipation rate is non-negative for all possible processes is overly restrictive. Presuming that requiring a non-negative dissipation rate is, in some sense, a stability requirement, a challenge is to develop a dissipation rate restriction that allows for a negative dissipation rate but that guarantees stability. This is of particular significance for discrete defect plasticity.

Although conditions to impose for maintaining stability in plasticity calculations where a negative dissipation rate occurs are not available, a simple one dimensional wave propagation calculation showed that it is possible for stability to be be maintained with a negative dissipation rate occurring for a short time, Needleman [9]. Fig. 1(a) shows an instability developing at t = 2.0 around x = 0.6 but that instability is damped out at a later times. An instability does develop around x = 0.6 in a calculation with a more negative dissipation rate, Fig. 1(b), so that there is a limit to a negative dissipation rate that can occur with stability maintained.

There is anecdotal evidence (i.e. seen in some calculations that I was involved in) that a negative dissipation rate can occur in a discrete defect plasticity calculation with no indications of an instability. The dissipation rate restriction required for stability to be maintained while one or more discrete events have a negative dissipation rate has not yet been developed.

In this regard, it should be mentioned, that there is the possibility that a full thermodynamic discrete defect plasticity framework, including temperature and entropy, is needed to provide a formulation that appropriately accounts for a negative dissipation rate. In such a framework, the Clausius-Duhem inequality, Eq. ( 8), can be satisfied even when the dissipation rate is negative. Whether or not a formulation that satisfies the Clausius-Duhem inequality but that gives a negative dissipation rate can maintain stability remains to be determined.

One use of discrete defect plasticity modeling is to develop the background for a higher level crystal plasticity constitutive relations and the consequence of allowing the possibility of a negative dissipation rate for individual discrete events for the higher scale modeling remains to be investigated. Quite generally there is a need for continuum mechanics formulations that maintain stability and, consistent with statistical mechanics, allow for a negative dissipation rate in a small region for a short time.

7 Concluding remarks 1. The Clausius-Duhem inequality emerges from a statistical description of interacting discrete entities in the limit of a large number of discrete entities interacting for a long time. It is not a fundamental physical requirement for all discrete events.

(i). In plasticity formulations, a restriction on dissipation rate can be phrased in terms of: (i) the pointwise dissipation rate ξ; or (ii) the volume integrated dissipation rate Ḋ. The requirement that ξ or Ḋ be non-negative for all possible deformation histories can be overly restrictive in some contexts. In such a context, the appropriate dissipation rate measure to restrict and the appropriate restriction to impose remain to be determined for various plasticity formulations.

2. The requirement in a purely mechanical theory of plasticity that the plastic dissipation rate is non-negative at each point of the body is related to, but not strictly derived from, the Clausius-Duhem inequality except in the special, and questionable, case of a plastic dissipative process with no change in entropy.
3. When plastic deformation is characterized by a collection of discrete events, the requirement that each discrete event gives rise to a non-negative dissipation rate can be overly restrictive.

(i) In particular, for discrete defect plasticity, the requirement of a non-negative dissipation rate for each discrete event imposes strong restrictions on discrete defect evolution equations that can preclude modeling experimentally observed and/or atomistically predicted processes.
4. When a negative dissipation rate is permitted for a small region for a short time, the requirement for ensuring stability remains to be developed.

figure.head: Figure 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 1: Plastic strain rate as a function of position at various normalized times for a one dimensional wave calculation. (a) Stability with a negative dissipation rate over a small region for a short time. (b) Instability with a larger negative dissipation rate. From Needleman [9].

note.p: "There have been nearly as many formulations of the second law as there have been discussions of it." P.W. Bridgman

div.head: Acknowledgment
div.p: I am pleased to thank Professor Ahmed Benallal of LMPS, Université Paris-Saclay, Centrale Supelec, ENS Paris-Saclay, CNRS for helpful comments and suggestions on a previous draft.

analytic.title: The thermodynamics of elastic materials with heat conduction and viscosity
The fluctuation theorem
Equalities and inequalities: irreversibility and the second law of thermodynamics at the nanoscale
Probability of second law violations in shearing steady states
Experimental demonstration of violations of the second law of thermodynamics for small systems and short time scales
Violations of the Clausius-Duhem inequality in Couette flows of granular media
Existence of entropy as a consequence of asymptotic stability
The second law of thermodynamics and stability
Discrete defect plasticity and implications for dissipation
Red herrings and sundry unidentified fish in nonlinear continuum mechanics
Nonequilibrium thermodynamics of amorphous materials II: Effective-temperature theory
Thermodynamic theory of dislocation-mediated plasticity
Deformation and failure of amorphous, solidlike materials
Nonequilibrium statistical thermodynamics of thermally activated dislocation ensembles: part 2-ensemble evolution toward correlation of enthalpy barriers
Nonequilibrium statistical thermodynamics of thermally activated dislocation ensembles: part 3-Taylor-Quinney coefficient, size effects and generalized normality
Some geometrical relations in dislocated crystals
A gradient theory of single-crystal viscoplasticity that accounts for geometrically necessary dislocations
A comparison of nonlocal continuum and discrete dislocation plasticity predictions
Guidelines for constructing strain gradient plasticity theories
Microstructurally based cross-slip mechanisms and their effects on dislocation microstructure evolution in fcc crystals
A molecular dynamics-informed probabilistic cross-slip model in discrete dislocation dynamics
Non-planar dislocation cores: a ubiquitous phenomenon affecting mechanical properties of crystalline materials
An atomistically-informed dislocation dynamics model for the plastic anisotropy and tension-compression asymmetry of BCC metals
Dislocation dynamics simulations of plasticity in Fe laths at low temperature
Incorporating three-dimensional mechanisms into two-dimensional dislocation dynamics
The determination of the elastic field of an ellipsoidal inclusion, and related problems
The elastic field outside an ellipsoidal inclusion
Energy dissipation rate and kinetic relations for Eshelby transformations
